papers.title: A new Hyper-Runaway star discovered from LAMOST and GAIA: ejected almost in the galactic rotation direction
papers.author_names: Yin-Bi Li A-Li Luo Gang Zhao You-Jun Lu Xue-Sen Zhang Fu-Peng Zhang Bing Du Fang Zuo Lan Zhang Yang Huang Mao-Sheng Xiang Jing-Kun Zhao Yong-Heng Zhao Zhan-Wen Hna

abstract.text: In this paper, we report the discovery of a new late-B type unbound hyper-runaway star (LAMOST-HVS4) from the LAMOST spectroscopic surveys. According to its atmospheric parameters, it is either a B-type main sequence (MS) star or a blue horizontal branch (BHB) star. Its Galactocentric distance and velocity are 30.3 +/- 1.6 kpc and 586 +/- 7 km/s if it is an MS star, and they are 13.2 +/- 3.7 kpc and 590 +/- 7 km/s if a BHB star. We track its trajectories back, and find that the trajectories intersect with the Galactic disk and the Galactic center lies outside of the intersection region at the 3 sigma confidence level. We investigate a number of mechanisms that could be responsible for the ejection of the star, and find that it is probably ejected from the Galactic disk by supernova explosion or multiple-body interactions in dense young stellar clusters.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A new hyper-runaway star (LAMOST-HVS4) is discovered with late-B type spectral characteristics, exhibiting high galactocentric velocity (586±7 km s⁻¹) and distance (30.3±1.6 kpc), indicating unbound status. Trajectory backtracking shows no intersection with the Galactic disk, ruling out ejection from the disk; instead, dynamical ejection mechanisms such as supernova explosions or multiple-body interactions in dense young stellar clusters are favored over Galactic center-origin scenarios.

source_fields.title: A new Hyper-Runaway star discovered from LAMOST and GAIA: ejected almost in the galactic rotation direction

source_fields.sections_text: 4. Summary: A new late-B type unbound hyper-runaway star (LAMOST-HVS4) is identified, likely ejected from a dense stellar cluster via supernova explosion or multiple-body interactions, with a trajectory nearly aligned to Galactic rotation and a galactocentric velocity of ~586 km s⁻¹. 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a refined analysis of hypervelocity stars using Gaia DR2 data, innovatively identifying and characterizing exceptions to the Galactic center origin scenario—particularly low-mass white dwarfs and helium-rich sub-dwarf O stars—by linking their kinematics and stellar properties to tidal disruption in binary systems or extragalactic origins, thereby solving the problem of understanding the diverse formation mechanisms behind HVSs beyond the traditional GC ejection models. 3. Possible Origin: LAMOST-HVS4 likely originated from the Galactic disk near the Sun, was ejected with a velocity consistent with a hyper-runaway star, and moved along a direction nearly aligned with the Galactic rotation. 2. The LAMOST survey and the new HVS: This section describes the discovery of a new hyper-runaway star, LAMOST-HVS4, identified in the LAMOST DR6-QI dataset with extreme radial velocity and late B-type spectral characteristics, suggesting it may be a hot main-sequence or blue horizontal branch star ejected nearly in the direction of Galactic rotation.

abstract: In this paper, we report the discovery of a new late-B type unbound hyper-runaway star (LAMOST-HVS4) from the LAMOST spectroscopic surveys.
title: A new Hyper-Runaway star discovered from LAMOST and GAIA: ejected almost in the galactic rotation direction

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly report discovery of a new hyper-runaway star (LAMOST-HVS4) with observational data from LAMOST and Gaia.

papers.title: Impact of the Galactic Disk and Large Magellanic Cloud on the Trajectories of Hypervelocity Stars Ejected from the Galactic Center
papers.author_names: Scott J. Kenyon Benjamin C. Bromley Warren R. Brown Margaret J. Geller

abstract.text: We consider how the gravity of the Galactic disk and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) modifies the radial motions of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center. For typical HVSs ejected towards low (high) Galactic latitudes, the disk bends trajectories by up to 30 degrees (3-10 deg). For many lines-of-sight through the Galaxy, the LMC produces similar and sometimes larger deflections. Bound HVSs suffer larger deflections than unbound HVSs. Gravitational focusing by the LMC also generates a factor of two overdensity along the line-of-sight towards the LMC. With large enough samples, observations can detect the non-radial orbits and the overdensity of HVSs towards the LMC. For any Galactic potential model, the Galactic rest-frame tangential velocity provides an excellent way to detect unbound and nearly bound HVSs within 10 kpc of the Sun. Similarly, the rest-frame radial velocity isolates unbound HVSs beyond 10-15 kpc from the Sun. Among samples of unbound HVSs, measurements of the radial and tangential velocity serve to distinguish Galactic Center ejections from other types of high velocity stars.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: hypervelocity stars, unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper quantifies how gravitational perturbations from the Galactic disk and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) alter the trajectories of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center, showing up to 30° deflections for low-latitude HVSs and significant focusing of HVSs toward the LMC that creates a factor-of-two overdensity. Bound HVSs experience larger deflections than unbound ones, and kinematic signatures—particularly rest-frame tangential and radial velocities—can distinguish Galactic Center-origin HVSs from other high-velocity populations, enabling detection beyond 10–15 kpc.

source_fields.title: Impact of the Galactic Disk and Large Magellanic Cloud on the Trajectories of Hypervelocity Stars Ejected from the Galactic Center

source_fields.sections_text: SUMMARY: Gravitational interactions with the Galactic disk and the LMC deflect hypervelocity stars' trajectories, with tangential and radial velocity measurements providing robust methods to identify unbound stars and constrain the Galactic potential. BACKGROUND: This paper advances the study of high-velocity star ejection mechanisms near supermassive black holes by investigating how external gravitational potentials, such as the Large Magellanic Cloud, induce observable anisotropies and trajectory modifications in the outflow, thereby extending beyond isotropic models to provide a more realistic framework for interpreting the 3D distribution of high-velocity stars in the Galactic halo. DISCUSSION: The Galactic disk and LMC significantly alter HVS trajectories, deflecting low-latitude stars by several degrees, concentrating bound HVSs toward the disk and creating a factor-of-two overdensity toward the LMC, which increases the inferred HVS rate from the GC by 20–40% and necessitates upward revisions in halo models. INTRODUCTION: This paper introduces a novel framework that combines dynamical simulations with observational data to distinguish between ejection mechanisms of high-velocity stars, specifically addressing the problem of identifying whether these stars originate from binary interactions, SMBH tidal disruptions, or galactic tidal streams by analyzing their velocity distributions and stellar properties. NUMERICAL CALCULATIONS: This section describes numerical simulations of hypervelocity star trajectories in a Milky Way plus Large Magellanic Cloud gravitational potential, tracking their 3D space motions, proper motions, and radial velocities over main sequence lifetimes to predict observable properties. RESULTS: HVS TRAJECTORIES: Hypervelocity stars ejected at larger angles to the Galactic plane experience significant deflections (up to ~8° for $v_0 = 900$ km/s and $\\phi_0 \\approx 15^\\circ$), with deflection magnitude decreasing with higher ejection velocities (down to ~1.5° for $v_0 = 1350$ km/s) and being sensitive to disk mass and scale parameters, where larger disk mass or smaller scale lengths enhance deflection and shift the peak deflection to smaller or larger angles, respectively. OBSERVATIONAL DISCRIMINANTS: The presence of the LMC slightly alters the positional and velocity distributions of hypervelocity stars by displacing them toward the LMC, but does not significantly affect their velocity dispersions or specific angular momenta compared to models without the LMC. IDENTIFYING UNBOUND HYPERVELOCITY STARS: Identifying unbound hypervelocity stars is most effective using tangential velocity at large distances, where radial velocity alone is insufficient for nearby stars due to velocity dispersion and orbital orientation biases.

abstract: We consider how the gravity of the Galactic disk and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) modifies the radial motions of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center.
Among samples of unbound HVSs, measurements of the radial and tangential velocity serve to distinguish Galactic Center ejections from other types of high velocity stars.
title: Impact of the Galactic Disk and Large Magellanic Cloud on the Trajectories of Hypervelocity Stars Ejected

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper is theoretical modeling of gravitational effects on HVS trajectories; mentions 'large enough samples' but presents no actual observational data or catalog.

papers.title: A quantitative spectral analysis of 14 hypervelocity stars from the MMT survey
papers.author_names: Andreas Irrgang Simon Kreuzer Ulrich Heber Warren Brown

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) travel so fast that they may leave the Galaxy. The tidal disruption of a binary system by the supermassive black hole in the Galactic center is widely assumed to be their ejection mechanism. To test the hypothesis of an origin in the Galactic center using kinematic investigations, the current space velocities of the HVSs need to be determined. With the advent of Gaia's second data release, accurate radial velocities from spectroscopy are complemented by proper motion measurements of unprecedented quality. Based on a new spectroscopic analysis method, we provide revised distances and stellar ages, both of which are crucial to unravel the nature of the HVSs. We reanalyzed low-resolution optical spectra of 14 HVSs from the MMT HVS survey using a new grid of synthetic spectra, which account for deviations from local thermodynamic equilibrium, to derive effective temperatures, surface gravities, radial velocities, and projected rotational velocities. Stellar masses, radii, and ages were then determined by comparison with stellar evolutionary models that account for rotation. Finally, these results were combined with photometric measurements to obtain spectroscopic distances. The resulting atmospheric parameters are consistent with those of main sequence stars with masses in the range 2.5 - 5.0 $M_\\\\odot$. The majority of the stars rotate at fast speeds, providing further evidence for their main sequence nature. Stellar ages range from 90 to 400 Myr and distances (with typical $1\\\\sigma$-uncertainties of about 10-15%) from 30 to 100 kpc. Except for one object (B711), which we reclassify as A-type star, all stars are of spectral type B. The spectroscopic distances and stellar ages derived here are key ingredients for upcoming kinematic studies of HVSs based on Gaia proper motions.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A quantitative reanalysis of 14 hypervelocity stars using non-LTE synthetic spectra improves atmospheric parameter determination, revealing main-sequence B-type stars with masses 2.5–5.0 M⊙, ages 90–400 Myr, and distances 30–100 kpc, with fast rotation confirming their main-sequence nature and enabling accurate kinematic origin studies in the Gaia era.

source_fields.title: A quantitative spectral analysis of 14 hypervelocity stars from the MMT survey

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach to spectral analysis of blue runaway stars by incorporating level dissolution in model atmospheres, enabling accurate determination of surface gravity from the Balmer jump region in MMT spectra—critical for obtaining precise spectroscopic distances to enable kinematic origin studies of Galactic halo runaways. The Gaia era: The Gaia mission will revolutionize stellar astrophysics and Galactic astronomy by providing unprecedented proper motion data, enabling precise three-dimensional velocity measurements and origin constraints for high-velocity stars through combined spectroscopic distances and kinematic analyses. Evolutionary masses and stellar ages: The stellar masses, ages, and rotational properties of the main sequence stars are derived from evolutionary models and atmospheric parameters, with masses between 2.5 and 5.0 M⊙ and rotation inferred from projected rotational velocity. Model atmospheres and synthetic spectra: The paper proposes a hybrid LTE/non-LTE spectral synthesis method (ADS approach) that combines ATLAS12 for atmospheric structure, DETAIL for non-LTE atomic populations, and SURFACE for spectrum calculation, with iterative feedback between departure coefficients and atmospheric structure to improve accuracy, particularly for B-type stars and the Balmer jump. Spectroscopic distances and interstellar reddening: Spectroscopic photometry enables the derivation of stellar angular diameters and interstellar reddening, which are used to determine distances—ranging from 10 to 100 kpc with ~10–15% uncertainties—by fitting synthetic spectral energy distributions to observed data. Atmospheric parameters and projected rotational velocities: This study derives precise atmospheric parameters, including effective temperature, surface gravity, radial velocity, and projected rotational velocity for a sample of stars using advanced spectral fitting, revealing significant discrepancies in temperature and gravity for a few objects, consistent rotational velocities with prior studies, and fast rotation indicating main-sequence nature. A quantitative spectral analysis of 14 hypervelocity stars from the MMT survey: This study presents a quantitative spectral analysis of 14 hypervelocity stars from the MMT survey to characterize their stellar properties and origins.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) travel so fast that they may leave the Galaxy.
The resulting atmospheric parameters are consistent with those of main sequence stars with masses in the range 2.5 - 5.0 $M_\\\\odot$.
The majority of the stars rotate at fast speeds, providing further evidence for their main sequence nature.
Except for one object (B711), which we reclassify as A-type star, all stars are of spectral type B.
title: A quantitative spectral analysis of 14 hypervelocity stars from the MMT survey

catalog_assessment.evidence: Explicitly analyzes 14 hypervelocity stars from the MMT HVS survey with spectroscopic data, atmospheric parameters, distances, ages; combines with Gaia proper motions.
